context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m trying to finish the Spider Diagram for my case study city in the climate action course, but I’m kind of unsure what information is supposed to go under each of the 5 pathways. Could you help me figure out how to organize it?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you work through this! Before we dive in — what do you already know about the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation from the course material? Even a rough idea of what they cover is a good starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for clarification on how to organize the Spider Diagram. Rather than explaining the 5 pathways directly, I should use guided discovery by asking them what they already know. This keeps the Socratic approach intact and helps me understand where they are starting from before providing any scaffolding.

exchanges.student: I only remember them kind of vaguely, not confidently. I think they’re different areas cities can act in, but I’m fuzzy on how to tell which policy or project belongs in which pathway.
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable starting point! Before we go further, I'd suggest going back to the course material where the 5 Pathways are introduced. Once you've reviewed them, come back and tell me what they are — even in your own words. That'll give us a solid foundation to work from.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a vague but partially correct intuition. Rather than filling in the gaps for them, I should prompt them to go back to the course material to refresh their understanding of the 5 pathways. This is a case where guided discovery means directing them to the right resource first, before we work on applying the concepts.

exchanges.student: Okay, I can do that. If I come back with a messy version of the five in my own words, can you help me sort out which kinds of city actions fit under each one?
exchanges.tutor: Absolutely — that's exactly the right approach. Come back with what you find and we'll work through it together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a reasonable and forward-looking question about the process. I can confirm this approach without giving anything away, and encourage them to come back with their attempt. This keeps the momentum going and sets clear expectations.

exchanges.student: Sounds good, thanks. I’ll pull them up and then come back with my rough notes.
exchanges.tutor: Great, take your time. See you when you're back!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stepping away to review the course material, which is exactly what I encouraged. A brief, supportive acknowledgment is appropriate here to keep the interaction warm without adding unnecessary content.

exchanges.student: Thanks — I’m hoping it’ll make more sense once I see the exact pathway names again.
exchanges.tutor: It usually does! Go take a look and come back when you're ready.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just making a casual remark before stepping away. A brief acknowledgment is fine, but I should gently nudge them to actually go look at the material rather than continuing to chat.

exchanges.student: Okay, I will. I was probably overthinking it a bit.
exchanges.tutor: Possibly! The material will clear things up. Go check it out.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still chatting rather than going to review the material. I should keep my response very short and redirect them to the task.

exchanges.student: Yep, I’ll check the course page and then bring back what I found.
exchanges.tutor: Sounds good — I'll be here when you're back with your notes!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps chatting instead of going to review the material. I should keep this very brief and stop engaging with the small talk to redirect them to the task.
Brief farewell, nothing more needed here.

exchanges.tutor: Bye! Come back with those pathway notes and we'll get to work.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still exchanging pleasantries. A very brief response is appropriate, but I should note we are using up exchanges without academic progress. When the student returns, I should quickly redirect to the task.

The student is still just exchanging pleasantries and we've now used several exchanges without any academic progress. I should stop engaging with the small talk entirely and wait for them to return with substantive content.
